name: Pinocchio (Rigid Body Dynamics Library)

performance.notes: C++ Eigen-based; extremely fast for real-time control (microseconds per call)

agent_readiness.notes: State-of-the-art rigid body dynamics for robotics and biomechanics. import pinocchio as pin. model, visual_model, collision_model = pin.buildModelsFromUrdf('robot.urdf'). data = model.createData(). q = pin.neutral(model). pin.forwardKinematics(model, data, q). pin.updateFramePlacements(model, data). oMi = data.oMi[joint_id]. SE3: pin.SE3(R, t). Jacobian: pin.computeJointJacobians(model, data, q); J = pin.getJointJacobian(model, data, joint_id, pin.ReferenceFrame.LOCAL_WORLD_ALIGNED). RNEA (inverse dynamics): tau = pin.rnea(model, data, q, v, a). ABA (forward dynamics): a = pin.aba(model, data, q, v, tau). Crocoddyl for optimal control.

gotchas: Documentation is sparse — primary resource is example code and research papers
URDF loading requires mesh files in correct relative paths
Configuration space q is Lie group (SO3, SE3) — not simple Euclidean vector for floating-base
No type annotations in Python bindings
Requires conda or manual build — not on PyPI for all platforms
best_when: Robot dynamics: forward/inverse kinematics, Jacobians, dynamics for control
Optimal control and trajectory optimization with Crocoddyl
Real-time robot control requiring microsecond-latency dynamics
avoid_when: Simple kinematic-only robots (use ROS tf2 or modern robotics library)
Simulation with contact/collision (use PyBullet or MuJoCo)
Beginners needing documentation — steep learning curve
summary: Pinocchio computes rigid body dynamics for robotics. buildModelsFromUrdf(). forwardKinematics(model, data, q). updateFramePlacements(). getJointJacobian(). RNEA for inverse dynamics. ABA for forward dynamics. SE3 Lie group geometry. Microsecond latency via C++ Eigen. BSD-2 by LAAS-CNRS. State-of-the-art for robotics dynamics and optimal control (Crocoddyl). Sparse docs. URDF+mesh loading. Lie group config space for floating-base robots.
